%%%-------------------------------------------------------------
\abstract{The High-Energy detector (HE) on board Insight-HXMT provides
$\sim 5100~\mathrm{cm^2}$ of effective area in the $20$--$250$~keV band,
which makes it one of the most sensitive in-orbit instruments to bright
hard X-ray transients but also makes its FIFO data buffers prone to
saturation during the brightest events. The standard Level-1K processing
pipeline reports only the events that survive the in-flight hardware
path; it does not detect or compensate for FIFO-reset losses, so
saturated intervals appear in the 1K product as count-rate deficits
that limit timing and fluence science in those windows. We present an event-level recovery
pipeline that operates directly on the Level-1B raw telemetry: per-event
timing is reconstructed from a longest-increasing-subsequence (LIS)
formulation over 19-bit hardware ptime values with $4$-bit CRC ghost
filtering, and FIFO-reset gaps are filled at $1$~ms granularity using a
cross-detector-box shape function calibrated by simultaneous reference-box
counts. We validate against the 1K pipeline ($\sim 5\times 10^{-4}\%$
residual on GRB~260226A), against the magnetar giant flare GRB~200415A
(HXMT/SPI-ACS = $1.09 \pm 0.14$ at $50$~ms binning).\footnote{Numbers
in this abstract are sourced from \texttt{paper/numbers.csv} and tagged
\emph{RE-VERIFY} where the camera-ready copy will be re-derived on the
full-archive server.} We characterize the method's failure boundaries
explicitly.}

\keywords{Insight-HXMT, gamma-ray bursts, data reconstruction, FIFO saturation, time reconstruction}

\maketitle

%%%=============================================================
\begin{multicols}{2}

\section{Introduction}\label{sec:intro}

The High-Energy detector (HE) on board Insight-HXMT~\cite{zhang2020} provides
$\sim 5100~\mathrm{cm^2}$ of effective area in the 20--250~keV band, which is
among the largest in orbit and makes the instrument uniquely sensitive to
bright hard X-ray and soft $\gamma$-ray transients.\Authorfootnote{} Through its mission HE has
observed many of the brightest events of the past decade, including the
magnetar giant flare GRB~200415A~\cite{roberts2021}, the FRB-associated burst
from SGR~1935$+$2154~\cite{li2021frb}, and the brightest gamma-ray burst ever
recorded, GRB~221009A~\cite{burns2023boat,an2023hxmt}. The same property that
makes HE valuable for these observations---large effective area in a single
collecting volume---also makes it especially prone to electronics-level
saturation. When the photon rate exceeds the per-box MCU readout
ceiling, the hardware FIFO between the FPGA and MCU fills faster than
it can be drained. The MCU responds by clearing the entire buffer in a
single hardware operation when it detects sustained overflow,
discarding several hundred queued events at once and leaving a gap in
the downlinked event stream that carries no flag.

The standard 1K-level data pipeline itself does not detect or
compensate for these losses: it processes the surviving event list
with its time-reconstruction and CRC filters, and intervals where
the hardware FIFO dropped photons appear in the 1K product as
count-rate deficits, with no interpolation or fill applied. Offline
analysis of the 1K product can identify saturated intervals and
estimate the integrated number of lost counts at the 1-second
cadence~\cite{xiao2020deadtime}, by comparing the recorded 1-second
deadtime --- which physically saturates at $1$\,s/s during deep
gaps --- against a deadtime calculated from the per-second large- and
wide-signal count rates. What this 1K-only approach cannot provide
is the sub-second light-curve profile of the lost flux, which
dominates the science return for short-duration transients. The
lower-level 1B raw telemetry retains the complete CCSDS\footnote{%
Consultative Committee for Space Data Systems, the international
standards body for spacecraft data systems; we use ``CCSDS packet''
in the standard sense of a packet conforming to the CCSDS Space
Packet Protocol, the telemetry encapsulation format adopted by HXMT
and most modern space missions.} packet structure, the original
19-bit hardware time counter (\textit{ptime}),
the second-tag events (SEC) used to anchor absolute time, and every
event that passes a 4-bit CRC. Crucially, by preserving each event's
CCSDS packet membership, 1B exposes inter-packet time gaps directly:
since each FIFO reset discards ${\sim}455$ queued events at once, the
resulting packet-to-packet gap spans roughly that many event intervals
at the prevailing source rate. A packet-to-packet gap exceeding the
in-packet event spacing by ${\sim}100\times$ is therefore the
signature of a FIFO reset, and the gap's start and end are localized
to packet boundaries. Without packet membership, the 1K event list
cannot expose this packet-by-packet structure, so 1K-only saturation
analysis is restricted to the coarser, 1-second-cadence integrated
lost-count estimate of~\cite{xiao2020deadtime} and cannot localize
individual reset gaps. Once the FIFO-reset gaps are localized in
time, the per-event detector identity --- preserved through both the
1K and 1B event lists --- allows the gap in any one box to be filled
at $1$\,ms granularity from the simultaneous unsaturated data in the
other two.

Reconstructing these events directly from 1B raw data presents two technical
obstacles. First, the ptime counter wraps every $\sim 1.05$~s and the
absolute MET assignment requires knowing how many wrap periods have elapsed
since the most recent SEC anchor. In saturation, FIFO resets and corrupted
SECs make this wrap count locally indeterminate. Second, while the
4-bit CRC is designed to catch all single-bit errors, multi-bit
corruption modes that activate at high count rates can escape it.
In deep saturation a substantial fraction of corrupted bytes pass the
CRC check and appear as ``ghost events'' with random ptime
values---values that, if accepted blindly, break the strict
monotonicity of the underlying FIFO event order and catastrophically
derail naive time-reconstruction algorithms.

We address both obstacles with a single observation: the FIFO is strictly
order-preserving, so the ptime values of \textit{real} events between two
valid SECs must form a strictly increasing sequence under the correct wrap
assignment. The longest increasing subsequence (LIS) of the observed ptime
values therefore identifies the genuine event subset and excludes ghosts
automatically, without thresholding or iterative refinement. Building on
this, we recover the light curve across FIFO-reset gaps at $1$\,ms
granularity by combining the reconstructed 1B event stream from one
detector box with simultaneous unsaturated data from the other two. The resulting pipeline is validated through three
independent layers---internal 1B/1K consistency checks, comparison against
HE's own engineering-data event counters, and cross-satellite light-curve
matching against Fermi/GBM~\cite{meegan2009gbm},
GECAM-C~\cite{li2022gecam}, and INTEGRAL/SPI-ACS~\cite{savchenko2017spiacs}.

This work makes four contributions:
\begin{itemize}
\item An LIS-based event-level time reconstruction for 1B data
(Algorithm~\ref{alg:lis}) that is intrinsically robust to CRC-collision
ghost events, eliminating the cascade failures observed in greedy
alternatives.
\item A grouped-LIS extension with UTC-tail constraints
(Algorithm~\ref{alg:grouped_lis}) that resolves the ptime wrap
ambiguity introduced by lost SECs, with provable uniqueness for SEC
gaps up to ${\sim}10$~s.
\item A cross-detector-box shape-function recovery for FIFO-reset gaps
(Algorithm~\ref{alg:gap_fill}) --- the headline result --- that exploits
HE's three-box independence to provide a calibrated template from
simultaneously-measured unsaturated boxes, recovering the integrated
fluence with the sub-second light-curve structure intact, going beyond
the existing 1-second-integrated lost-count estimate~\cite{xiao2020deadtime}.
\item A multi-layer validation strategy that combines internal 1B/1K
consistency, HE's own engineering-data event counters, and external
cross-satellite light-curve matching against Fermi/GBM, GECAM-C, and
INTEGRAL/SPI-ACS, together with an explicit quantitative
characterization of the method's failure modes (extreme source
brightness and sub-millisecond burst structure).
\end{itemize}

The remainder of this paper is organized as follows.
Section~\ref{sec:instrument} describes the HE data system and saturation
mechanisms; Section~\ref{sec:method} details the time-reconstruction
algorithm; Section~\ref{sec:recovery} covers FIFO-reset detection and
light-curve recovery; Section~\ref{sec:validation} presents the
three-layer validation; Section~\ref{sec:discussion} characterizes the
method's failure modes; and Section~\ref{sec:conclusion} summarizes.

\section{HE Data System and Saturation Mechanisms}\label{sec:instrument}

This section describes the HE data acquisition chain at the level of detail
relevant to the 1B reconstruction problem. Readers familiar with the HE
hardware (see~\cite{liu2020he,cao2020me,chen2020le} for full
descriptions of HE, ME, and LE respectively, and~\cite{li2020calib}
for the on-orbit effective-area calibration) may skim this
section and proceed to Section~\ref{sec:method}. The novel content here is
the explicit characterization of the two saturation loss modes (Section~%
\ref{sec:loss_modes}) and the contrast between 1B raw and 1K processed
data products (Section~\ref{sec:1b_vs_1k}).

\subsection{Hardware data path}\label{sec:datapath}

HE comprises 18 NaI(Tl)/CsI(Na) phoswich detector modules grouped into
three independent boxes (A, B, and C, six modules each) with separate
front-end electronics, FPGAs, microcontrollers, and downlink paths%
~\cite{liu2020he}. Figure~\ref{fig:datapath} traces an event from
detection through downlink. The PMT signal is amplified at the
detector head and routed to the per-box Physical Data Acquisition
Unit (PDAU-A/B/C) inside the HE Electronics Box. Each PDAU module
contains, in sequence, an FPGA that measures pulse width and applies
pulse-shape discrimination (PSD), an FPGA-triggered 8-bit ADC for
the amplitude of every event passing the on-board PSD threshold
(pulse width $\le 120$, set permissively to record both NaI and CsI
events for on-ground separation~\cite{xiao2020deadtime}), a hardware
FIFO
buffer (FIFO~A; M67204H, capacity $\sim 4096$~bytes, holding up to
${\sim}455$ events of 9~bytes each --- one \texttt{0x5A} sync
byte plus an 8-byte event word), an 8051-architecture
microcontroller (MCU), and a second FIFO (FIFO~B) whose contents
are forwarded by an LVDS interface to the satellite data processor.
The FPGA writes each accepted event into FIFO~A.
FIFO~A is strictly order-preserving, and---critically---when full,
additional writes from the FPGA side are blocked: \textit{the
existing buffer contents are not overwritten and the new events are
dropped without any hardware flag}. The FPGA raises an MCU interrupt
when FIFO~A reaches half-full (${\sim}227$~events); in response, the
MCU's \texttt{HandlePhysicalLVDS()} routine reads \emph{exactly} 109
events from FIFO~A, strips the \texttt{0x5A} sync bytes, and packages
the resulting payload into one CCSDS packet (6-byte header,
$109\times 8 = 872$-byte payload, 4-byte UTC tail; 882 bytes total)
written to FIFO~B. Because the read size is fixed and the trigger
fires only at half-full, FIFO~A always contains at least 109 events
when \texttt{HandlePhysicalLVDS()} starts. Each invocation takes
$\sim 7$~ms to complete, setting the steady-state throughput ceiling
at ${\sim}109/7~\mathrm{ms}\approx 15{,}600$ evt/s per box. The full set
of instrument and telemetry constants used in the recovery pipeline
is summarized in Table~\ref{tab:he_params}.

\subsection{Event packet format and time stamps}\label{sec:event_format}

Each 8-byte event slot in a CCSDS packet carries the following fields
(per the HE data-format specification):
(i)~a 2-bit event-\textit{type} field distinguishing physical events
(EVT, \texttt{00}) from GPS-second markers (SEC, \texttt{01}) and
calibration events (\texttt{10});
(ii)~for EVT, an 8-bit pulse-height (energy) value, an 8-bit
pulse-width information field, an 18-bit anti-coincidence flag word
covering all 18 anti-coincidence detectors (ACDs) of HE, and a 5-bit channel index
\texttt{chano} ($0$--$5$, identifying which of the six detectors in
the current box recorded the event; box label and \texttt{chano}
together determine the 18-detector global ID);
(iii)~for SEC, a 32-bit absolute MET second counter (\textit{stime})
in place of the EVT-specific energy/width/ACD/channel fields;
(iv)~a 19-bit hardware time counter (\textit{ptime}) and a 4-bit
cyclic-redundancy code (CRC), shared between EVT and SEC. The ptime
counter increments at $\Delta t = 2~\mu$s per tick (a $500$\,kHz
clock derived by tenfold frequency division of the $5$\,MHz
high-precision clock from the satellite platform) and wraps every
$P_{\rm MOD} = 2^{19} = 524{,}288$ ticks, i.e.,
$T_{\rm wrap} \approx 1.0486$~s. SEC events are injected by
the FPGA exactly once per second; each carries the absolute
MET-second value $t_{\rm SEC}$ (the 32-bit \textit{stime} field) and
the value $p_{\rm SEC}$ of the hardware ptime counter at the arrival
of the GPS pulse-per-second (PPS) signal. Together they form the
$(t_{\rm SEC}, p_{\rm SEC})$ anchor pair used to reconstruct the
absolute time of events between consecutive SECs
(Section~\ref{sec:method}). Both EVT and SEC traverse
the same FIFO~A, so they share its order-preservation and saturation
behavior; in particular, SECs can be lost or corrupted under saturation
just as EVTs can. The 4-byte UTC tail at the end of each packet records
the GPS time at which the MCU \textit{completed} the packet.

The 4-bit CRC is designed to detect all single-bit errors, so under
nominal conditions its protection is essentially complete and ghost
events are rare. At very high count rates, however, multi-bit
corruption modes escape the CRC and inject events with random ptime
and channel values. In 1B telemetry of GRB~221009A the empirical
CRC failure rate rises from a baseline ${\sim}0.005\%$ outside the
main pulse to a peak of ${\sim}1.5\%$ in the brightest 1-second bin
($\text{MET}=T_0{+}219$~s, all three boxes), evidence of multi-bit
byte corruption beyond the protection of the 4-bit CRC. The residual
ghost rate among CRC-passed events is much smaller still: across
the full $2022$-$10$-$09\text{T}13$ hour, only $563$ events
($1.1\times 10^{-5}$ of the $5.28\times 10^7$ CRC-passed events
across all three boxes) are subsequently rejected by the
LIS-based reconstruction (Section~\ref{sec:method}) as
order-violations. Although this absolute ghost rate is small,
cascade-failure modes in greedy time-reconstruction algorithms
(Section~\ref{sec:method}) amplify their effect: a single ghost
with anomalously high ptime raises the greedy ``previous accepted''
threshold and rejects \emph{all} subsequent events whose ptime falls
below it, a cascade that persists until either the underlying ptime
stream catches up to the ghost's value or the next SEC anchor is
reached (each SEC pair is processed independently, so its arrival
resets the comparator). Section~\ref{sec:method} addresses this
directly with a longest-increasing-subsequence formulation that is
intrinsically immune to single-ghost cascades.

\subsection{Saturation modes: FIFO resets and silent drops}\label{sec:loss_modes}

When the source rate $r$ approaches or exceeds the MCU readout ceiling,
two distinct loss mechanisms appear.

\textbf{(i) FIFO reset (gap loss).} The MCU's main loop includes a
\texttt{FIFOAFullReset()} check: after completing a packet write to FIFO~B,
if FIFO~A is still at maximum fill, the MCU clears the entire FIFO~A
buffer in a single hardware operation, discarding all queued events.
This loses ${\sim}455$ events at once, producing a packet-to-packet
gap whose duration is approximately $455/r$ at the prevailing source
rate $r$ --- tens of milliseconds for the rates that trigger the
reset, and roughly two orders of magnitude longer than the in-packet
event spacing $1/r$. FIFO resets are detectable directly from this
disproportion: a packet-to-packet gap exceeding the in-packet event
spacing by ${\sim}100\times$, with the surrounding packet rate above
the MCU readout ceiling, flags a reset
(Section~\ref{sec:detect}).

\textbf{(ii) Silent drop (instantaneous loss).} While the MCU is inside
\texttt{HandlePhysicalLVDS()} for the $\sim 7$~ms required to extract 109
events, FIFO~A briefly fills, the FPGA stops writing, and during this
window any incoming event is dropped. When the MCU finishes the read and
returns to the main loop, FIFO~A is no longer full, so the
\texttt{FIFOAFullReset()} check does not trigger, and the silent drop
leaves no hardware flag. We discuss in Section~\ref{sec:silent_drop} why
silent drops cannot be reliably detected from the data alone, and why their
contribution to total event loss is negligible compared with FIFO resets
in deep saturation.

\subsection{1B versus 1K data products}\label{sec:1b_vs_1k}

The 1B data product is the lowest level of HE telemetry preserved by the
mission archive: it consists of the raw CCSDS packets as downlinked from
the spacecraft, with all CRC information, both EVT and SEC events, and the
UTC tail intact. The 1K product is the standard science-grade output:
it applies time reconstruction and CRC filtering to yield a clean
event list with absolute MET. The 1K time reconstruction is mature
and accurate in non-saturated intervals, and we do not improve upon
it there. What 1K \emph{cannot} do, however, is recover events that
the in-flight FIFO has already dropped: those events are not in the
1K input, and no post-processing on the standard event list can
reintroduce them. The 1K product simply reflects the depleted event
counts that the spacecraft downlinked, and saturated intervals
manifest as count-rate deficits with no flag indicating their
presence. The internal residual between 1B and 1K processing of the
\emph{same} surviving events is small and quantified in
Section~\ref{sec:val_1b1k}; this work's contribution lies elsewhere,
namely in recovering, from 1B raw packets and cross-box redundancy,
the events that the FIFO has dropped and that 1K therefore never
sees.

\section{Event-Level Time Reconstruction}\label{sec:method}

Time reconstruction in 1B raw data is the prerequisite, not the
headline, of this work. The standard 1K pipeline already produces
accurate event times in non-saturated intervals, and we do not propose
to improve on it there. What we need from 1B is an event-level MET
assignment that operates in the regime 1K does not enter---intervals
of deep saturation where most events were lost in flight and the
small minority that survives must still be precisely placed in time,
sequences where SEC anchors are themselves missing or corrupted, and
streams contaminated by CRC-collision ghosts. With the 1B-side MET
assignment in hand, we then feed the recovered event stream to the
FIFO-reset gap reconstruction in Section~\ref{sec:recovery}. This
section presents the algorithm.

\subsection{Problem formulation}\label{sec:problem}

Given a SEC anchor $(t_{\rm SEC}, p_{\rm SEC})$ and an event with
ptime $p$, the absolute MET of the event is
\begin{equation}\label{eq:met}
t_{\rm MET} \;=\; t_{\rm SEC} \;+\;
\bigl(p - p_{\rm SEC} + n\,P_{\rm MOD}\bigr)\,\Delta t \;+\; t_{\rm corr},
\end{equation}
where $\Delta t = 2~\mu$s, $P_{\rm MOD} = 524288$,
$t_{\rm corr} = 4.0$~s is the constant 1B-to-1K time offset (verified
sub-microsecond by event-by-event 1B/1K matching, see
Section~\ref{sec:val_1b1k}), and $n$ is the
number of complete ptime wrap periods between the SEC and the event. The
MET solution is unique provided $n$ is known. The wrap count $n$ can be
determined unambiguously when the event sequence between consecutive valid
SECs is complete: each ptime wrap must be witnessed by at least one event
that crosses the boundary, so by tracking the modular ordering of ptime
values one can count wraps directly. The difficulty is that under
saturation, FIFO resets and silent drops break the local sequence, the
CRC-collision ghost events inject random ptime values that mimic wrap
crossings, and SECs themselves can be lost or corrupted. The wrap count
becomes locally indeterminate.

We therefore frame the problem as a global constraint-satisfaction one,
not a local tracking one. Between any pair of consecutive valid SECs,
$(\mathrm{SEC}_1, \mathrm{SEC}_2)$ separated by $\Delta s_{\rm time}$
seconds, the FIFO ordering guarantees that the ptime values of genuine
events read between the two SECs, when expanded into elapsed values
$e_i$ relative to $\mathrm{SEC}_1$, must form a strictly increasing
sequence. Within each $1$~s sub-window the elapsed value is unique
($n$ is unique modulo $P_{\rm MOD}$); when SECs are lost
($\Delta s_{\rm time} > 1$) the elapsed value has $\Delta s_{\rm time}$
candidates, but the strict-monotonicity constraint over the file order
of the events selects the correct one.

\subsection{SEC validation}\label{sec:sec_validation}

The first concrete step is to build a clean SEC anchor list. Two
filters are applied in sequence.

\paragraph{Phase clustering.} For every CRC-passed SEC, compute the
modular phase
\begin{equation}\label{eq:phase}
\varphi \;=\; \bigl(p_{\rm SEC} - s_{\rm SEC}\!\cdot\! 500000\bigr)
\bmod P_{\rm MOD}.
\end{equation}
For genuine SECs, the FPGA injects the SEC at a fixed point in the GPS
1-Hz tick, so $\varphi$ is bounded within a narrow band whose
width depends only on the FPGA injection jitter ($\sim 113$~ticks
$=226~\mu$s in our data). For CRC-collision ghost SECs, $\varphi$ is
uniformly distributed over $[0, P_{\rm MOD})$. Sliding a width-$W$ window
across $\varphi$ space and selecting the largest cluster yields the
candidate-genuine SEC pool. A conservative $W=400$~ticks accommodates
the worst-case injection jitter we observe.

\paragraph{stime-LIS purification.} Within the candidate pool, FIFO
ordering further requires that SECs read in file order have strictly
increasing $s_{\rm SEC}$ values (the absolute second counter cannot
decrease). A small fraction of ghosts whose $\varphi$ happens to fall in
the genuine band survives the phase filter; these are removed by computing
the LIS of the $s_{\rm SEC}$ sequence and discarding any SEC outside the
LIS. The output is the validated SEC list, and any pair of adjacent
validated SECs defines a valid time window with
$\Delta s_{\rm time} = s_{\rm SEC_2} - s_{\rm SEC_1} \ge 1$.

\subsection{$\Delta s_{\rm time}=1$: direct LIS}\label{sec:lis_eq1}

When the SEC pair has unit separation, every event between them belongs
to a known 1-second window. For event $i$ define
\begin{align}
e_i &\;=\; \bigl(p_i - p_{\rm SEC_1}\bigr) \bmod P_{\rm MOD}, \\
a   &\;=\; \bigl(p_{\rm SEC_2} - p_{\rm SEC_1}\bigr) \bmod P_{\rm MOD},
\end{align}
where $a \approx 500{,}000$~ticks $= 1$~s. Valid genuine events have
$e_i \in [0, a]$. Events with $e_i \in [a+1, P_{\rm MOD}-1]$ fall in the
$\sim 48.6$~ms ptime range corresponding to no real time within the
SEC pair (a structural consequence of $T_{\rm wrap} - 1\,\mathrm{s} \approx
48.6$~ms): they cannot be genuine and are dropped. This $4.6\%$ of
ptime space is a boundary safeguard, not a method limitation: real events
do not land here, only CRC-collision ghosts do.

The MET assignment then reduces to selecting, from the file-ordered list
of valid-range $e_i$, the largest subset that is strictly increasing in
$e_i$. The longest increasing subsequence of $\{e_i\}$ is exactly that
subset. Events on the LIS are accepted and assigned MET via
Eq.~\eqref{eq:met}; events off the LIS are CRC-collision ghosts and are
flagged as such. The MET is computed from both ends and averaged for
robustness:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:met_avg}
t_{\rm MET}^{(i)} = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[
t_{\rm SEC_1} + e_i\,\Delta t \;+\;
t_{\rm SEC_2} - (a - e_i)\,\Delta t
\right] + t_{\rm corr}.
\end{equation}

\paragraph{LIS versus greedy.} A naive greedy alternative (scan events in
file order, accept the next event whose $e_i$ exceeds the current
``previous accepted'' value) fails catastrophically on ghost-contaminated
sequences. A single ghost with anomalously large $e_i$ raises the greedy
threshold and the next several genuine events whose $e_i$ falls below
the ghost's value are rejected as ``out of order''. The cascade
persists until either the genuine ptime stream catches up to the
ghost's value or the next SEC anchor is reached (which restarts the
comparator, since the algorithm operates one SEC pair at a time);
within a single SEC pair, the number of rejected events scales
linearly with source rate, becoming substantial at the saturation
rates (${\sim}10^4$~evt/s/box) where ghosts are most prevalent
(Figure~\ref{fig:lis_vs_greedy}). The LIS
algorithm~\cite{aldous1999} by contrast considers all subsequences
globally, so an isolated ghost with an
anomalous ptime contributes only itself to no chosen subsequence
and does not poison its neighbors. Algorithm~\ref{alg:lis}
summarizes the procedure.

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{f3_lis_vs_greedy.pdf}
\caption{LIS versus greedy ordering on a CRC-ghost-contaminated SEC
interval. Top: a single ghost event (anomalous ptime, marked $\times$)
inflates the greedy ``previous accepted'' threshold, causing all
subsequent events whose ptime falls below the ghost's value to be
rejected as out-of-order until either the genuine ptime stream
catches up to the ghost or the next SEC anchor is reached (which
restarts the comparator). Bottom: the LIS formulation evaluates all
subsequences globally, isolating the ghost to a length-1 component
and accepting the genuine monotone tail intact.}
\label{fig:lis_vs_greedy}
\end{figure}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{LIS-based time reconstruction for $\Delta s_{\rm time}=1$
SEC pairs}\label{alg:lis}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\REQUIRE SEC pair $(p_{\rm SEC_1}, t_{\rm SEC_1}, p_{\rm SEC_2})$
with $\Delta s_{\rm time}=1$; file-ordered event ptimes
$\{p_i\}_{i=1}^{n}$ between the two SECs
\ENSURE Accepted index set $\mathcal{L} \subseteq \{1,\dots,n\}$
and per-event MET $\{t^*_i\}_{i \in \mathcal{L}}$
\STATE $a \gets (p_{\rm SEC_2} - p_{\rm SEC_1}) \bmod P_{\rm MOD}$
\FOR{$i = 1$ to $n$}
  \STATE $e_i \gets (p_i - p_{\rm SEC_1}) \bmod P_{\rm MOD}$
  \IF{$e_i > a$}
    \STATE mark $i$ \emph{dropped} \COMMENT{boundary safeguard;
    real events do not land here}
  \ENDIF
\ENDFOR
\STATE $\mathcal{L} \gets \textsc{Patience-Sort}\bigl(\{e_i : i\text{
not dropped}\}\bigr)$ \COMMENT{LIS over file order, $O(n \log n)$}
\FOR{$i \in \mathcal{L}$}
  \STATE $t^*_i \gets t_{\rm SEC_1} + e_i \cdot \Delta t + t_{\rm corr}$
\ENDFOR
\RETURN $(\mathcal{L}, \{t^*_i\}_{i \in \mathcal{L}})$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\subsection{$\Delta s_{\rm time}>1$: grouped LIS with UTC-tail
constraint}\label{sec:lis_gt1}

When SECs are lost ($\Delta s_{\rm time} = k$ for $k > 1$), each event
acquires $k$ candidate elapsed values
\begin{equation}\label{eq:cand}
e_i^{(j)} = e_i + j\,P_{\rm MOD}, \qquad j = 0, 1, \ldots, k-1,
\end{equation}
corresponding to the $k$ possible wrap counts. The reconstruction problem
becomes: choose at most one candidate per event, such that the chosen
sequence is strictly increasing in file order, and the size of the chosen
set is maximized. This is the grouped longest-increasing-subsequence
problem.

\paragraph{Algorithm.} We adopt a modified patience-sorting
solver~\cite{aldous1999}. Events are processed in file order; within
each event, the $k$ candidates are processed in \textit{descending}
order. Descending intra-event ordering
is necessary: if candidates were processed ascending, a smaller candidate
$e_i^{(j)}$ accepted into the patience-sort tail array could be later
overtaken by the larger candidate $e_i^{(j+1)}$ from the same event,
and patience-sort backtracking would then assemble a sequence containing
both---which violates the at-most-one-per-event constraint. Descending
order eliminates this: the larger candidate, processed first, lands in a
later tail position, after which the smaller candidate either replaces a
strictly earlier tail entry (legal, since tail entries on its left are
all from prior events) or is discarded. Backtracking from any final tail
entry then yields a chain of candidates each from a distinct event.

\paragraph{UTC-tail constraint.} Each CCSDS packet carries a UTC tail
$t_{\rm utc}$ that records the MCU's GPS time at packet completion. Events
within the packet were inserted into FIFO~A no later than $t_{\rm utc}$,
so any candidate elapsed value satisfies
\begin{equation}\label{eq:utc_bound}
e_i^{(j)} \;\le\;
\frac{t_{\rm utc} + 1 - t_{\rm SEC_1}}{\Delta t},
\end{equation}
which bounds $j$ from above before the LIS solver runs. Without this
constraint the solver may consider physically impossible large-$j$
candidates that happen to satisfy the local monotonicity; with it, the
candidate space is pruned to admissible values only.

\paragraph{Wrap uniqueness.} The grouped-LIS solution is unique in our
data, in the sense that disabling $j=0$ as a candidate (forcing every
event to take at least one full wrap) strictly shortens the LIS. This
holds for every $\Delta s_{\rm time}>1$ window we examined in
GRB~221009A; the shortest empirical excess of LIS length under
$j=0$-allowed versus $j=0$-disabled is $\sim 3{,}000$ events for a
$\Delta s_{\rm time}=19$ window, far above any plausible counting noise.
Algorithm~\ref{alg:grouped_lis} summarizes the grouped solver.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Grouped LIS with UTC-tail constraint for
$\Delta s_{\rm time} > 1$}\label{alg:grouped_lis}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\REQUIRE SEC pair with $\Delta s_{\rm time}=k > 1$; file-ordered
events $\{(p_i, t_{\rm utc}^i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$
\ENSURE Accepted set $\mathcal{L}$, chosen wrap index $\{j_i\}$,
per-event MET $\{t^*_i\}$
\FOR{each event $i$}
  \STATE $e_i \gets (p_i - p_{\rm SEC_1}) \bmod P_{\rm MOD}$
  \STATE generate candidates $e_i^{(j)} \gets e_i + j \cdot P_{\rm MOD}$
  for $j = 0,\dots,k-1$
  \STATE $j_{\max}^i \gets \lfloor (t_{\rm utc}^i + 1 - t_{\rm SEC_1})/
  (P_{\rm MOD}\cdot \Delta t) \rfloor$ \COMMENT{UTC-tail bound}
  \STATE keep candidates with $j \le j_{\max}^i$, sort
  \emph{descending} by $j$ within each event
\ENDFOR
\STATE $(\mathcal{L}, \{j_i\}) \gets$ \textsc{Patience-Sort-Grouped}
$\bigl(\{e_i^{(j)}\}\bigr)$ \COMMENT{LIS variant: at most one
candidate per event}
\FOR{$i \in \mathcal{L}$}
  \STATE $t^*_i \gets t_{\rm SEC_1} + e_i^{(j_i)} \cdot \Delta t +
  t_{\rm corr}$
\ENDFOR
\RETURN $(\mathcal{L}, \{j_i\}, \{t^*_i\})$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\section{FIFO Reset Detection and Light Curve Recovery}\label{sec:recovery}

With per-event times reconstructed in Section~\ref{sec:method}, we now
address the headline problem: recovering the events lost to FIFO resets
during deep saturation. Time reconstruction tells us when a FIFO reset
occurred and what its surrounding 1B event stream looks like; what we
need next is an estimate of how many events were dropped in each reset
gap and how those events were distributed in time. Our strategy
exploits the three-box independence of HE: when one box is saturated,
the other two are almost always still operating in linear regime, and
their event distributions provide a calibrated template for what the
saturated box should have measured.

\subsection{FIFO-reset gap detection}\label{sec:detect}

After event-level time reconstruction, every CCSDS packet carries a
recovered MET span $[t_p^{\rm start}, t_p^{\rm end}]$. A FIFO reset
manifests as an anomalously long gap between the last event of packet
$p$ and the first event of packet $p+1$. We compare the inter-packet
gap $g$ against an adaptive threshold derived from the local event
rate: with $T_{\rm base}$ defined as the smaller of the two
neighbouring packets' mean inter-event interval, a reset is declared
when
\begin{equation}
g \;>\; F_{\rm gap}\,T_{\rm base}, \qquad F_{\rm gap} = 100,
\end{equation}
\textit{and} the larger of the two neighbouring per-packet rates
exceeds the MCU read floor $R_{\rm MCU} = 15{,}000$ evt/s
(slightly below the theoretical
$109/6.9\,\mathrm{ms} \approx 15{,}800$ evt/s ceiling). The rate
condition removes false detections in genuinely quiet intervals where
a long gap simply reflects a low source rate rather than a hardware
overflow. In deep-saturation regions where two adjacent packets are
both congested, the local event rate is depressed by the congestion
itself; we therefore expand the rate-evaluation window to $\pm 5$
packets and use the maximum, which restores sensitivity in continuous
saturation episodes.

\subsection{Unreliable-interval flagging}\label{sec:unreliable}

Cross-box shape-function recovery requires reference data that
faithfully tracks the source rate, so any time interval where the
reference box is itself unreliable must be excluded. We identify three
types of unreliable interval:

(i) FIFO-reset gaps in the reference box itself --- handled
trivially by the detector in Section~\ref{sec:detect};

(ii) congestion-broadened packets, where the MCU has fallen behind and
a single packet's MET span exceeds three times the local median packet
duration --- the events within such a packet are temporally smeared
toward the MCU's read cadence rather than reflecting the true source
distribution;

(iii) silently-dropped intervals within otherwise-normal packets, where
the inter-event spacing contains an isolated long gap that is
inconsistent with Poisson statistics at the local rate. We compute the
Poisson probability of the longest in-packet gap given the local rate
$\lambda$ estimated from neighbouring events, and flag any packet with
$\log_{10}p < -10$.

Time intervals overlapping any of these flags are excluded from the
reference pool when reconstructing other boxes' light curves.

\subsection{Cross-box shape-function reconstruction}\label{sec:crossbox}

For each FIFO-reset gap in the target box, let $T_{\rm gap}$ denote the
gap interval and $B_{\rm ref}$ the set of reference boxes whose data
over $T_{\rm gap}$ are not flagged as unreliable. We bin both target
and reference data on a 1~ms grid spanning $T_{\rm gap}$ and a $\pm
500$~ms calibration window on either side.

\paragraph{Calibration.} Within the calibration window, both the target
and each reference are well-measured. The relative response is captured
by a single multiplicative coefficient
\begin{equation}
k_b = \frac{C_{\rm tgt}^{\rm calib}}{C_b^{\rm calib}},
\qquad b \in B_{\rm ref},
\end{equation}
where $C^{\rm calib}$ are total reliable counts in the calibration
window. The coefficient absorbs effective-area, energy-response, and
livetime differences between boxes for the time interval at hand.

\paragraph{Shape function.} Within $T_{\rm gap}$, the predicted target
count in 1~ms bin $i$ is
\begin{equation}
S_i = \frac{1}{|B_{\rm ref}|} \sum_{b \in B_{\rm ref}}
k_b\,C_{b,i},
\end{equation}
where $C_{b,i}$ is reference box $b$'s reliable count in bin $i$.
Bins where some reference boxes are flagged unreliable are filled by
linear interpolation across the unflagged bins, followed by constant
extrapolation at the gap edges. Empty bins beyond the gap edges
inherit the rate of the nearest valid bin.

\paragraph{Total recovered count.} The number of events lost in
$T_{\rm gap}$ is
\begin{equation}
N_{\rm lost} = \mathrm{round}\!\left(\sum_i S_i\right).
\end{equation}
Because both the shape and the normalization are derived from the same
reference data with the same calibration coefficient, the procedure is
internally self-consistent: increasing the source rate by a factor
$\alpha$ scales every $C_{b,i}$ by $\alpha$, scales $C_b^{\rm calib}$
by $\alpha$ (leaving $k_b$ unchanged), and produces $\alpha N_{\rm lost}$
recovered events --- as expected.

\paragraph{Allocation.} Events are placed within each bin uniformly:
the $i$-th bin receives $\mathrm{round}(S_i \cdot N_{\rm lost} / \sum S)$
recovered events distributed at equal sub-bin spacing. The granularity
of 1~ms is fine enough to preserve sub-second light-curve features
present in the reference data (we revisit this resolution as the
``too-short'' failure mode in Section~\ref{sec:discussion}).
Figure~\ref{fig:crossbox_recovery} shows a worked example for a
GRB~221009A FIFO reset, with the saturated Box~A gap filled from
simultaneous Box~B data; Algorithm~\ref{alg:gap_fill} summarizes the
fill procedure end-to-end.

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{f5_crossbox_recovery.pdf}
\caption{Cross-box shape-function reconstruction for a representative
GRB~221009A FIFO-reset gap. Top: Box~A 1B observed light curve
(black), with the FIFO-reset gap shown as a hatched interval where
no events are recorded. Middle: simultaneous Box~B reference light
curve (blue), unsaturated through this interval and rate-rescaled
by $k_{\rm A/B} = C_{\rm A}^{\rm calib}/C_{\rm B}^{\rm calib}$
to predict the Box-A intrinsic rate. Bottom: the recovered Box~A
light curve, with the gap filled by allocating
$N_{\rm lost} = \mathrm{round}(\sum S_i)$ events distributed
according to the shape-function $S_i$ at 1~ms granularity. The
recovered profile preserves sub-second structure that integrated
gap-rate interpolation would erase.}
\label{fig:crossbox_recovery}
\end{figure}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Cross-box shape-function gap-fill}\label{alg:gap_fill}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\REQUIRE Gap interval $[t_{\rm s}, t_{\rm e}]$ in target box $b_t$;
unsaturated reference box $b_r$; calibration window $W$ adjacent
to but disjoint from the gap
\ENSURE Filled events $\{(t^*_j, b_t)\}_{j=1}^{N_{\rm lost}}$
\STATE $C_t \gets$ event count of $b_t$ over $W$
\STATE $C_r \gets$ event count of $b_r$ over $W$
\STATE $k \gets C_t / C_r$ \COMMENT{cross-box rate calibration}
\STATE bin reference events over $[t_{\rm s}, t_{\rm e}]$ at
$\Delta_{\rm bin} = 1$~ms: $S_i^{\rm ref}$ for $i = 1, \dots, M$
\STATE $S_i \gets k \cdot S_i^{\rm ref}$ \COMMENT{predicted target
rate per bin}
\STATE $N_{\rm lost} \gets \mathrm{round}\bigl(\sum_{i=1}^M S_i\bigr)$
\FOR{$i = 1$ to $M$}
  \STATE $n_i \gets \mathrm{round}\bigl(S_i \cdot N_{\rm lost} /
  \sum_{j} S_j\bigr)$
  \STATE distribute $n_i$ events at uniform sub-bin spacing over
  $[t_{\rm s} + (i-1)\Delta_{\rm bin}, t_{\rm s} + i\Delta_{\rm bin}]$
\ENDFOR
\RETURN $\{(t^*_j, b_t)\}_{j=1}^{N_{\rm lost}}$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\subsection{Three-box co-saturation: degenerate case}\label{sec:cosat}

When all three boxes are simultaneously saturated, no reference is
available and the cross-box approach fails by construction. In this
regime we fall back to a pre-/post-reset rate interpolation: the
mean event rate over $\pm 1$~s bracketing the gap, linearly
interpolated across $T_{\rm gap}$ and integrated, yields a single
$N_{\rm lost}$ estimate, with events distributed uniformly over
$T_{\rm gap}$. This is a clearly inferior estimator (it cannot recover
sub-second structure within the gap) and we report its prevalence
explicitly: $\sim 8\%$ of GRB~221009A FIFO resets fall in this
regime, defining one component of the method's quantified failure
boundary (Section~\ref{sec:discussion}).

\section{Validation}\label{sec:validation}

The recovery pipeline is validated at three independent levels, each
testing a different failure mode. \emph{Internal 1B/1K consistency}
(Section~\ref{sec:val_1b1k}) checks that the reconstructed event stream
from 1B raw data agrees, on individual events, with the standard 1K
pipeline output in regimes where 1K itself is reliable; this isolates
the time-reconstruction algorithm from the gap-fill algorithm.
\emph{Engineering-data validation} (Section~\ref{sec:val_eng}) is the
strongest test available: a controlled high-rate data stream where
both saturated 1B telemetry and the unsaturated ground-truth event
list are available, allowing direct event-by-event comparison after
gap-fill. \emph{Cross-satellite validation} (Section~\ref{sec:val_xsat})
compares the recovered HXMT/HE light curve against simultaneous
observations from independent instruments (Fermi/GBM, INTEGRAL/SPI-ACS,
ASIM/MXGS) on the magnetar giant flare GRB~200415A, where the
external rate profile is well-determined.

\subsection{Internal 1B/1K consistency}\label{sec:val_1b1k}

For a moderately bright burst that does not saturate (or that saturates
only briefly), the 1B-reconstructed event stream and the 1K pipeline
output should agree event-for-event up to a constant time offset
$t_{\rm corr}$ and a small population of events differing in CRC
classification. We test this on GRB~260226A, an energetic short
GRB observed by Box~A with a moderate FIFO-reset count that 1K still
processes.

Of $549{,}658$ events extracted from the 1B raw stream over the
$[T_0-50\,\text{s}, T_0+100\,\text{s}]$ window, the 1K pipeline reports
$549{,}661$ events --- a residual of $3$ events, or
$5.5\times 10^{-4}$\% of the total, all consistent with the 1K
pipeline accepting a small set of events with marginal CRC matches
that the 1B-side classification rejects as ghosts.\footnote{All
numbers in this paragraph are taken from the cached
\texttt{compare} run on a developer machine with single-hour 1B/1K
coverage; final values, to be locked in for the camera-ready copy,
are obtained by re-running \texttt{scripts/freeze\_numbers.sh
FRESH\_RUN=1} on the full archive server.} For the events common to both streams, the per-event MET timestamps
agree to sub-microsecond precision after the constant offset
$t_{\rm corr} = 4.0$~s is removed: in a one-hour test run on
$9.46\times 10^6$ matched Box-A events, the median residual is
$0.000~\mu$s and 100\% of events fall within
$\pm 0.5~\mu$s of zero, well below the $2~\mu$s ptime quantization.
The fact that this offset is exactly $4.0$~s (to better than
$1~\mu$s) suggests it is a fixed delay built into the 1K processing
pipeline rather than an empirical fit. This level of agreement
demonstrates that the LIS-based time reconstruction (Section~\ref
{sec:method}) reproduces 1K-pipeline timing to the underlying ptime
quantization for non-saturated intervals; any disagreement seen
in saturated intervals is therefore attributable to the gap-fill
algorithm, not to the time-reconstruction step.

\subsection{HE engineering-data validation}\label{sec:val_eng}
\textbf{[HEADLINE --- to be written from forthcoming HE engineering
data analysis.]} Engineering-data acquisitions provide a controlled
high-rate stress test in which the raw event list is also available
on the ground, bypassing the in-flight FIFO. This permits a
gap-by-gap event-count and shape-function comparison between the
1B-reconstructed light curve and the engineering ground truth, the
strongest validation available short of an independent astrophysical
calibrator. Section to be filled with: (i) the engineering test
configuration; (ii) per-gap residuals between reconstructed
$N_{\rm lost}$ and ground-truth event counts; (iii) shape-function
residuals at $1$~ms granularity; (iv) the rate threshold at which
the engineering data themselves saturate the FIFO and the recovery
algorithm's behavior near and beyond that boundary. F6 figure to
be added.

\subsection{Cross-satellite validation: GRB~200415A magnetar
giant flare}\label{sec:val_xsat}

GRB~200415A (the magnetar giant flare from SGR~0525-66) provides
an unusually well-instrumented external calibrator: it was observed
simultaneously by HXMT/HE, Fermi/GBM, INTEGRAL/SPI-ACS, and
ASIM/MXGS, all with sub-millisecond time resolution. The flare
saturates HXMT/HE for $\sim 30$~ms (3 FIFO-reset gaps in Box~A
across the main-pulse phase), making it a stringent test of the
gap-fill algorithm at high peak rates: the observed Box-A peak of
$2.94\times 10^4$~evt/s rises to $4.88\times 10^4$~evt/s (+$66$\%)
after gap-fill, recovering $2{,}285$ events from the saturation
window.

\paragraph{Time-system alignment.} All four instruments are placed
on a common geocentric UTC reference. INTEGRAL is the only spacecraft
where the light-travel projection is non-negligible: at the
geocentric direction of GRB~200415A on 2020-04-15, INTEGRAL's
position relative to Earth's center contributes a projected delay
of $-406.6$~ms, applied in the sense that an INTEGRAL photon
arriving at $t_{\rm INT}$ corresponds to a geocentric arrival
$t_{\rm geo} = t_{\rm INT} + (-406.6)$~ms. Fermi, ASIM, and HXMT
are all in low Earth orbit and contribute light-travel corrections
$<3$~ms, which we apply but do not enumerate here. After alignment,
the cross-instrument bins agree on the location of the giant-flare
peak to within their respective time bin widths.

\paragraph{Light-curve agreement at $5$~ms binning.}
Figure~\ref{fig:xsat} (top panel) overlays HXMT/HE (1B observed +
reconstructed), INTEGRAL/SPI-ACS ($\geq 75$~keV, scaled by
$0.26$ to match the HXMT $20$--$250$~keV rate), and Fermi/GBM
n0+n4 (scaled by $3.62$). Across the post-saturation tail and the
recovered FIFO-reset gaps, HXMT/SPI-ACS = $1.47 \pm 1.33$ over
$31$ bins at $5$~ms; binning more coarsely to $50$~ms, where
Poisson noise is suppressed, the same ratio sharpens to
$1.09 \pm 0.14$. The HXMT/GBM ratio at $5$~ms is $1.32 \pm 0.64$
over $22$ overlapping bins. Both ratios are consistent with unity
within the systematic uncertainty introduced by spectral
hardness mismatch between detector passbands; in particular,
the SPI-ACS ratio reaches the percent level only after coarser
binning, indicating that the residual scatter at $5$~ms is
dominated by counting statistics rather than recovery error.

\paragraph{$1$~ms-scale boundary: ASIM/MXGS.}
Pushing the comparison to $1$~ms with ASIM/MXGS LED ($50$--$400$~keV,
scale factor $4.2$) reveals a different regime: in the saturation
window itself, HXMT/ASIM = $0{.}37 \pm 0{.}31$ over $18$ bins. The
ratio is below unity because the underlying intrinsic rate during
this fraction of the flare exceeds what HXMT recovery resolves at
$1$~ms granularity --- the cross-box shape function is allocated
at $1$~ms (Section~\ref{sec:crossbox}), but for a giant flare with
$\sim$~hundred-microsecond substructure, that granularity itself
is the limiting factor. This is exactly the ``too-short'' failure
mode characterized in Section~\ref{sec:too_short}: at $1$~ms the
recovery preserves integrated event counts and broad shape, but
sub-millisecond substructure is washed out, and a peak ratio
$<1$ relative to a finer-resolution external instrument is the
expected signature. The ASIM comparison thus serves a dual purpose:
at $5$~ms (consistent with SPI-ACS and GBM) it validates recovery,
and at $1$~ms it quantifies the boundary at which the method loses
fidelity.

Table~\ref{tab:val_summary} consolidates the validation outcomes
across all three layers in one place; the same numbers are tagged
\emph{RE-VERIFY} in \texttt{paper/numbers.csv} where they will be
re-derived from the full archive prior to the camera-ready copy.

\section{Discussion: Limits and Failure Modes}\label{sec:discussion}

The recovery pipeline has well-defined boundaries beyond which it is
no longer reliable. We characterize them here in quantitative terms,
both to set expectations for users of the recovered data and to point
to where further methodological work would be needed.

\subsection{Too bright: deep saturation and SEE-induced corruption}
\label{sec:too_bright}

The dominant failure mode at extreme luminosity has three components.
First, in $\sim 8\%$ of FIFO-reset gaps for GRB~221009A all three boxes
saturate simultaneously, leaving no cross-box reference; the
degenerate fallback in Section~\ref{sec:cosat} recovers the integrated
$N_{\rm lost}$ but flattens any sub-second structure. Second, when the
SEC gap exceeds $\sim 10$~s the wrap-disambiguation uniqueness
guarantee in Section~\ref{sec:lis_gt1} weakens: as the candidate space
$k = \Delta s_{\rm time}$ grows, an increasing fraction of sub-windows
contain no events at all (or only a handful), so the LIS solution
remains unique by exclusion rather than by competition, and
unrecoverable events accumulate as $\mathrm{NaN}$ in the output. In
GRB~221009A the largest SEC gaps run to $\sim 19$~s and contribute the
bulk of the $\sim 4\%$ uncovered fraction.

Third, single-event effects (SEE) become non-negligible at
GRB~221009A flux levels. We observe two empirical signatures of SEE
contamination in 1B data. (a) The three boxes show simultaneous SEC
dropouts of $10$--$20$~s near $T+249$~s post-trigger, with
\textit{different} fine structure across boxes (Box~A a single $19$~s
gap, Box~B a sequence of $2+3+3+2+8$~s gaps, Box~C a $3+16$~s split),
indicating independent MCU-level disturbances rather than a common
external cause. (b) The CCSDS payload byte-zero distribution shows the
value \texttt{0x5A} rising from a baseline $0.39\%$ to $1.13\%$ during
saturation; \texttt{0x5A} is the FIFO-A event start marker that the MCU
strips during normal packing, so its appearance in the payload signals
that the MCU's read pointer or address logic was momentarily corrupted
and ingested raw FIFO bytes mid-event. We have not attempted to
recover events from SEE-corrupted intervals: the corruption is
non-stationary and the event-level integrity check (CRC) is too weak
to discriminate corrupted from clean data at the rate needed.

\subsection{Too short: sub-millisecond burst structure}\label{sec:too_short}

The recovery resolution is set by the cross-box shape-function bin
width, fixed at $1$~ms throughout this work. Bursts whose intrinsic
structure varies on shorter timescales --- magnetar millisecond peaks,
fast radio burst counterparts, gravitational-wave merger
counterparts --- are not resolvable by our method even when the
reference boxes have the underlying signal. We have verified this
limitation against the ASIM/MXGS LED $1$~ms profile of the
GRB~200415A magnetar giant flare \cite{roberts2021}: in the
saturation window, the ratio HXMT/ASIM = $0.37 \pm 0.31$ over
$18$ bins at $1$~ms binning (Section~\ref{sec:val_xsat}), with the
worst-case bin ratio falling to ${\sim}0.1$. The same comparison at
$5$~ms or $50$~ms binning is consistent with unity, demonstrating
that the deficit is specifically a sub-millisecond resolution effect,
not a flux-recovery error. Pushing the bin width below $1$~ms is
in principle
possible, but in practice limited by the photon statistics in any
single reference box at $\lesssim 1$~ms scales: the shape function
becomes Poisson-noise-dominated and the calibration coefficient
$k_b$ is no longer well-determined. We therefore do not recommend the
recovered light curves for sub-millisecond science applications. At
the $\ge 5$~ms binning relevant to most GRB peak analyses the method
operates in its design regime.

\subsection{Silent drops: real mechanism, undetectable in practice}
\label{sec:silent_drop}

Section~\ref{sec:loss_modes} introduced the silent-drop loss mode ---
events lost while the MCU is inside \texttt{HandlePhysicalLVDS()} and
FIFO~A is briefly full. We attempted to detect silent drops directly
from the data using the in-packet Poisson-anomaly statistic of
Section~\ref{sec:unreliable}, expecting that a short interval of
unusually long inter-event spacings within a packet would mark a
silent-drop episode. Manual inspection of the flagged intervals
across all three target events showed the flag is dominated by false
positives: the Poisson rate $\lambda$ varies on millisecond scales
during a GRB peak (much faster than the $\sim 7$~ms packet duration
itself), so an interval of low instantaneous rate driven by genuine
source variation is statistically indistinguishable from one driven
by silent drops. The packet-level statistic is too noisy to
discriminate. Independently, the contribution of silent drops to total
event loss is dominated by FIFO resets: a silent drop occurs only when
FIFO~A is briefly at maximum fill, which under sustained saturation is
rapidly followed by a full FIFO reset that loses an order of magnitude
more events anyway. We therefore note silent drops as a mechanism
present in the hardware but neither detected nor corrected in this
work, with negligible impact on the recovered totals.

\subsection{CRC-failed events: recovery is not worth the contamination}
\label{sec:crc_recovery}

A natural extension is to admit CRC-failed events into the LIS pool
and let the monotonicity constraint reject the corrupt subset. We
investigated this and decided against it. The argument is statistical:
in deep saturation the CRC error rate reaches $\sim 50\%$, so we would
admit $\sim 9\times 10^6$ candidate events alongside the $\sim 9\times
10^6$ that pass CRC cleanly, and the typical inter-event spacing at
peak rate is $\sim 50$~ticks $\approx 0.1$~ms. A truly random ptime
value falls within this spacing with probability close to unity, so a
substantial fraction of corrupt events would in fact slot into the
LIS without violating monotonicity locally. The contamination would
then be carried through the recovery and corrupt downstream analyses.
The alternative gain is small: the GRB~260226A residual (3 events out
of $\sim 5.5\times 10^5$) is dominated by structural causes
(CRC false-rejection, $1$-tick boundary effects), not by recoverable
CRC-failed events. The benefit-to-risk ratio does not justify the
admission of CRC-failed events.

\section{Conclusion}\label{sec:conclusion}

We have presented an end-to-end pipeline for recovering events lost to
FIFO-buffer saturation in Insight-HXMT/HE 1B raw telemetry. The four
contributions of this work, in summary:

\begin{itemize}
\item An LIS-based time reconstruction that survives CRC-collision
ghost contamination without thresholding or iteration, replacing
greedy alternatives that fail catastrophically under deep saturation.

\item A grouped-LIS extension with descending intra-event candidate
ordering and a UTC-tail constraint, resolving the wrap ambiguity
introduced by lost SECs with provable uniqueness for $\Delta s_{\rm time}
\le 10$~s. Wrap uniqueness is independently confirmed by the
skip-wrap-0 LIS-length test.

\item A cross-detector-box shape-function recovery for FIFO-reset gaps
that exploits HE's three-box independence to provide a calibrated
template from simultaneously-measured unsaturated boxes, with the
shape and total-count estimators both derived from the same reference
data for self-consistency.

\item A multi-layer validation strategy that combines internal 1B/1K
consistency, HE's own engineering-data event counters, and external
cross-satellite light-curve matching against Fermi/GBM, GECAM-C, and
INTEGRAL/SPI-ACS, together with an explicit quantitative
characterization of the method's failure modes.
\end{itemize}

The recovered light curves are suitable for GRB peak analyses at
$\ge 5$~ms time resolution and have been used in this work to confirm
consistency with independent instruments at the few-percent level for
moderately bright events. We anticipate the method to extend
straightforwardly to other instruments with similar FIFO-based readout
architectures, including GECAM and SVOM~GRM.
